title: ACC SRVY 2343 — Surveying Legal Principles I

lessons.title: Week 1: Introduction to Property Law and the Surveyor's Role
learning_objectives: Explain why a land surveyor must understand the legal framework of property ownership, not merely measurement.
Identify the sources and hierarchy of Texas law and the structure of the Texas court system.
Distinguish the surveyor's role (locating boundaries) from the attorney's role (opining on ownership).
key_takeaways: Property law defines who owns what; surveying locates where that ownership lies on the ground.
Texas law is hierarchical: Constitution, statutes (codes), administrative rules (TAC), then case law.
Boundary and title disputes are civil matters that end at the Supreme Court of Texas, not the Court of Criminal Appeals.
Surveyors locate boundaries and flag conflicts; they do not render title opinions or practice law.

content.html:
Why a Surveyor Studies Law

A boundary survey is not merely a measurement exercise. The surveyor is asked to locate, on the ground, the limits of a legal interest in land created by deeds, grants, and statutes that may be centuries old. The measurement is the easy part; the hard part is deciding which evidence of a boundary the law treats as controlling. A surveyor who ignores the legal hierarchy of evidence—monuments over distances, senior rights over junior—can produce a mathematically perfect survey that is legally wrong, and a legally wrong boundary is the most expensive mistake a surveyor can make.

Property law answers who owns what and what bundle of rights ownership includes; surveying answers where that ownership lies. The two disciplines meet at the boundary line. This course gives you the legal foundation that the Surveyor-in-Training (SIT) and RPLS examinations demand, with constant attention to how Texas law differs from general common-law practice.

Sources and Hierarchy of Texas Law

Texas law is layered, and a higher source controls a lower one. In descending authority: (1) the Texas Constitution of 1876 (the current constitution); (2) statutes, organized into topical codes published at statutes.capitol.texas.gov; (3) administrative rules in the Texas Administrative Code (TAC); and (4) case law (common law applied through stare decisis). The codes most relevant to boundary work are the Property Code (estates, conveyances, recording, deeds, easements), the Natural Resources Code (public lands and the General Land Office), and the Civil Practice & Remedies Code Chapter 16 (adverse possession and the limitations period for suits against surveyors).

A classic exam trap: adverse possession is in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code, not the Property Code. Surveyor licensing flows from the Occupations Code Chapter 1071 (the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act), with conduct standards adopted by the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS) in 22 TAC Chapter 138.

rows: Public lands, the GLO, patents, navigable streams

content.html:
The Texas Court System

Trial courts of general jurisdiction are the District Courts; title and boundary disputes—trespass-to-try-title and quiet-title actions—begin there (Tex. Const. art. V, §8). Below them are County Courts (constitutional and statutory "county courts at law"), Justice (JP) Courts, and Municipal Courts. Appeals go to the intermediate Texas Courts of Appeals. Texas then has two co-equal highest courts: the Supreme Court of Texas, the final authority for civil cases (so boundary and title cases end there), and the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals, final only for criminal cases. Texas shares this split-apex design with only one other state, Oklahoma.

content.text: Texas differs: Most states and the federal system have a single court of last resort hearing both civil and criminal appeals. Texas splits this in two. Because boundary/title disputes are civil, the apex authority for surveying precedent is the Supreme Court of Texas; the Court of Criminal Appeals never touches boundary law.

content.html:
The Surveyor's Lane vs. the Attorney's Lane

A surveyor locates boundaries; a surveyor does not adjudicate ownership or render legal opinions on title. When you retrace a boundary you reconstruct where the original line was run and marked, applying legal rules of evidence (dignity of calls, footsteps of the original surveyor) to physical and record evidence. Deciding who owns a disputed strip, quieting title, or interpreting an ambiguous reservation is the work of a court and counsel. The professional skill is staying in your lane: report what the evidence shows the boundary to be, flag conflicts and gaps/overlaps, and route the legal questions to the parties' attorneys. Rendering a title opinion or otherwise practicing law without a license exposes the surveyor to liability and to TBPELS discipline.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Property law defines ownership; surveying locates it on the ground; they meet at the boundary line.
The Texas legal hierarchy is Constitution → statutes (codes) → administrative rules (TAC) → case law.
Boundary/title suits begin in District Court and end at the Supreme Court of Texas (a civil matter).
Adverse possession lives in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16, not the Property Code — a common exam trap.
Surveyors locate boundaries and flag conflicts; they do not render title opinions or practice law.

flashcards.definition: From highest to lowest authority: the Texas Constitution (1876), statutes (topical codes), administrative rules (Texas Administrative Code), and case law (common law / stare decisis).
flashcards.hint_1: Four layers; the Constitution is on top.

flashcards.definition: The statutory civil action in Texas used to determine title to and possession of real property; it begins in a District Court (general jurisdiction).
flashcards.hint_1: The Texas vehicle for boundary/title disputes.

flashcards.definition: Texas's design of two co-equal highest courts: the Supreme Court of Texas (final for civil cases, including boundary/title) and the Court of Criminal Appeals (final for criminal cases). Shared only with Oklahoma.

lessons.title: Week 2: Estates and Ownership in Real Property
learning_objectives: Describe the bundle of rights and the principal freehold and future estates recognized in Texas.
Distinguish fee simple absolute from defeasible fees and life estates.
Explain which common-law estate rules Texas has abolished by statute (Prop. Code §5.042).
key_takeaways: Ownership is a bundle of separable rights: possess, use, exclude, transfer, and profit.
Texas presumes a conveyance passes the entire fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (§5.001).
Defeasible fees leave a future interest: a possibility of reverter or a power of termination.
Texas abolished the Rule in Shelley's Case and the Doctrine of Worthier Title by §5.042.

content.html:
The Bundle of Rights

Ownership of land is best understood as a bundle of rights rather than a single thing: the right to possess, to use, to exclude others, to transfer (sell, lease, gift, devise), and to profit. These sticks can be separated. An owner may sell the surface but reserve the minerals, grant an easement (a right of use) without surrendering possession, or lease the land while keeping the fee. In Texas the mineral estate is frequently severed and is the dominant estate—the mineral owner may use as much of the surface as is reasonably necessary to develop the minerals. A surveyor must know which sticks a particular survey is being asked to locate.

Freehold Estates

The largest estate is fee simple absolute—ownership of potentially infinite duration with no inherent condition. Defeasible fees are fee estates that may end on the happening of an event: a fee simple determinable uses durational words ("so long as," "until") and automatically reverts, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter; a fee simple subject to condition subsequent uses conditional words ("but if," "provided that") and leaves the grantor a right of re-entry / power of termination that must be exercised. A life estate lasts for the measuring life (it may be pur autre vie, measured by another's life); the life tenant owes a duty not to commit waste. Future interests include the remainder (in a third party) and the reversion (retained by the grantor).

Texas presumes that a conveyance passes the entire estate (fee simple) unless a lesser estate is expressly limited—Tex. Prop. Code §5.001 ("unless the estate is limited by express words or unless a lesser estate is conveyed").

rows: Fee simple subject to condition subsequent
Right of re-entry / power of termination

content.text: Texas differs: Tex. Prop. Code §5.042 abolishes the Rule in Shelley's Case, the rule forbidding a remainder to the grantor's heirs, the Doctrine of Worthier Title, and a homestead-lien rule — giving such limitations effect according to grantor intent. Note: §5.042 does NOT abolish the destructibility of contingent remainders; Texas rejects that doctrine by case law, not by §5.042.

sections.title: Why estates matter to a surveyor
sections.content: A surveyor locates the physical extent of an estate, not its quality, but the kind of estate shapes the survey. A severed mineral estate may require depicting unit boundaries; a life estate plus remainder means two owners must be considered for access and signatures; a defeasible fee's condition can revest title and change who the abutting owner is. When the deed limits a lesser estate or reserves rights, the surveyor must read those words carefully because they define what bundle of sticks the boundary encloses.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Ownership is a bundle of separable rights (possess, use, exclude, transfer, profit).
Fee simple absolute is the largest estate; defeasible fees end on an event and leave a future interest.
Texas presumes a conveyance passes the whole fee unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (§5.001).
Texas abolished Shelley's Case and Worthier Title by §5.042 — but NOT destructibility of contingent remainders.
In Texas the severed mineral estate is the dominant estate over the surface.

flashcards.definition: A defeasible fee created with durational language ('so long as,' 'until') that automatically reverts to the grantor upon the stated event, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter.

flashcards.term: Possibility of reverter vs. right of re-entry
flashcards.definition: A possibility of reverter follows a fee simple determinable and revests title automatically; a right of re-entry (power of termination) follows a fee simple subject to condition subsequent and must be affirmatively exercised by the grantor.

Statute (eff. 1984) abolishing the Rule in Shelley's Case, the Doctrine of Worthier Title, the rule against a remainder to the grantor's heirs, and a homestead-lien rule, giving effect to such limitations per the grantor's intent.

lessons.title: Week 3: Deeds — Construction, Types, and Interpretation
learning_objectives: Distinguish general warranty, special warranty, and quitclaim deeds and the warranties each carries.
Apply the Texas four-corners / intent rule when construing a deed.
Explain why a Texas quitclaim grantee is generally not a bona fide purchaser, and the 2021 §13.006 cleansing window.
key_takeaways: A general warranty deed warrants against all defects; a special warranty only against the grantor's own acts; a quitclaim conveys only whatever interest the grantor has.
Texas implied covenants (§5.023) cover only no-prior-conveyance and no-encumbrances — not that the grantor actually owns the land.
Deeds are construed by the intent expressed within the four corners, harmonizing all parts.
A Texas quitclaim grantee is generally NOT a bona fide purchaser, though §13.006 (2021) adds a four-year cleansing window for downstream purchasers.

content.html:
The Three Workhorse Deeds

Three deed types dominate Texas conveyancing. A general warranty deed warrants title against all defects, reaching back through the entire chain of title; the grantor must defend against any lawful claim "whomsoever." A special warranty deed warrants only against defects arising "by, through, or under" the grantor—not against earlier links in the chain. A quitclaim deed conveys only whatever interest, if any, the grantor happens to have, with no warranty; it is not a muniment of title and does not even assert that the grantor owns anything.

Texas Statutory Form and Implied Covenants

Tex. Prop. Code §5.022 supplies the statutory warranty-deed form using the operative words "grant, sold, and conveyed" and "warrant and forever defend … against every person whomsoever lawfully claiming." A warranty, however, is not required (§5.022(b)). Tex. Prop. Code §5.023 says the words "grant" or "convey" imply only two covenants: (1) the grantor has not previously conveyed the same estate to another, and (2) the estate is free from encumbrances. Notably, §5.023 does not imply that the grantor actually owns the property—the broad protection of a general warranty comes from the express "warrant and forever defend" language, not from the implied covenants.

content.text: Texas differs: At common law the word 'grant' implied a covenant of seisin (that the grantor actually owns the estate). Texas §5.023 deliberately narrows the implied covenants to no-prior-conveyance + no-encumbrances. Broad title protection comes only from the express warranty language of §5.022.

content.html:
Construing a Deed: Intent Within the Four Corners

The controlling objective in construing a deed is the intent of the parties as expressed within the four corners of the instrument, read in light of the surrounding circumstances, harmonizing all parts so that none is rendered meaningless. Modern Texas deed-construction cases (e.g., Bass v. Harper, 441 S.W.2d 825 (Tex. 1969)) emphasize this four-corners, harmonize-the-whole method. When provisions truly cannot be harmonized, a specific (metes-and-bounds) description controls over a general one (see Concho Resources, Inc. v. Ellison (Tex. 2021)). The dignity-of-calls hierarchy you study in Week 7 is a subordinate tool used to find intent when the descriptive language conflicts.

sections.title: The Texas quitclaim peculiarity (and the 2021 statutory softening)
sections.content:
Classically, a grantee under a quitclaim (and successors) is generally NOT a bona fide purchaser: the quitclaim is treated as notice that the grantor's title may be defective, so the grantee takes subject to prior outstanding claims even though value was paid without actual notice (Woodward v. Ortiz, 237 S.W.2d 286 (Tex. 1951); Houston Oil Co. of Texas v. Niles, 255 S.W. 604 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1923)). Whether an instrument is really a quitclaim turns on the granting language taken as a whole, not the label—a deed that conveys the property itself may support BFP status while one conveying only the grantor's "right, title, and interest" does not (Cook v. Smith, 174 S.W. 1094 (Tex. 1915); Geodyne Energy Income Prod. P'ship I-E v. Newton Corp., 161 S.W.3d 482 (Tex. 2005)). A quitclaim "is not a conveyance or a muniment of title" (Rogers v. Ricane Enters., 884 S.W.2d 763 (Tex. 1994)).

2026 update: Tex. Prop. Code §13.006 (eff. Sept. 1, 2021) provides that after the fourth anniversary of recording, a quitclaim no longer affects the good faith of, or gives notice to, a subsequent purchaser or creditor. So for quitclaims recorded on/after Sept. 1, 2021, there is now a four-year cleansing window. The classic rule still applies to the immediate grantee and during the first four years—teach the two together.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: General warranty = defends all defects; special warranty = only grantor's own acts; quitclaim = grantor's interest 'if any.'
Texas implied covenants (§5.023) are only no-prior-conveyance and no-encumbrances — not seisin.
Deeds are construed by intent within the four corners, harmonizing every part.
A specific (metes-and-bounds) description controls over a general one when they cannot be reconciled.
A Texas quitclaim grantee is generally not a BFP; §13.006 (2021) adds a four-year cleansing window downstream.

flashcards.definition: A deed in which the grantor warrants title only against defects arising 'by, through, or under' the grantor — not against earlier links in the chain of title.
flashcards.hint_1: Narrower than general; only the grantor's own acts.

flashcards.definition: A deed conveying only whatever interest the grantor has, if any, with no warranty. It is not a muniment of title, and its grantee is generally not a bona fide purchaser (subject to the §13.006 four-year cleansing window for recordings after Sept. 1, 2021).

The Texas rule that a deed is construed according to the intent expressed within the four corners of the instrument, harmonizing all of its parts; a specific description controls over a general one when they conflict.

lessons.title: Week 4: Legal Descriptions — Metes and Bounds
learning_objectives: List the required elements of a Texas metes-and-bounds description.
Explain the Texas convention of tying a description to the original GLO survey and abstract number.
Apply the current TBPELS standard (22 TAC Ch. 138) and avoid citing the repealed Ch. 663.
key_takeaways: A metes-and-bounds description states a Point of Beginning, runs continuous bearing-distance calls around a closed perimeter, ties to monuments, and returns to the POB.
Texas descriptions are anchored to an original GLO survey + abstract number, not a PLSS grid.
Monuments control over bearings and distances; stated area is the least reliable element.
Current standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subch. E; the old Ch. 663 was repealed and is outdated for 2026.

content.html:
What a Metes-and-Bounds Description Must Do

A metes-and-bounds description must unambiguously identify a single closed parcel. It starts at a defined Point of Beginning (POB), runs a continuous series of measured calls—each a bearing and distance (e.g., "N 12°30' E, 210.50 ft")—around the perimeter, ties those calls to monuments, and returns to the POB so the figure mathematically closes. A description that does not close is legally defective. The governing standard is 22 TAC §138.91, which requires "a definite and unambiguous identification of the location of such boundaries" and that the description "describe all monuments found or placed."

rows: County, original GLO survey + abstract number, parent-tract record reference, acreage
Origin of the perimeter, tied to a corner of the original survey
Direction + length for each course, with a stated basis of bearing
Natural (creeks, trees) and artificial (iron rods, fences); state which found vs. placed
Acreage — the least reliable element; a check, not a control
Calls must return to the POB forming a closed figure

content.html:
The Texas Anchor: Original Survey and Abstract Number

Because Texas land was never federal public domain, every description is located within—and tied to—the original land-grant survey. A Texas caption almost always recites the grantee and abstract number, e.g., "situated in the H. & T.C.R.R. Co. Survey, Abstract No. ___, [County] County, Texas." The POB and at least one controlling corner tie back to a corner of that original survey so the parcel can be relocated independently of the surveyor's own work. Bearings reference a stated basis of bearing (22 TAC §138.91(c))—today typically the Texas State Plane Coordinate System on NAD83; historic descriptions used magnetic or assumed bearings, a known source of rotation discrepancies in retracement.

content.text: Texas differs: PLSS states anchor a description to a section/township/range corner. Texas anchors to an original land-grant survey + abstract number, administered by the General Land Office (a state, not federal, body). You cannot read a Texas description in township/range terms.

Do not cite 22 TAC Chapter 663. After HB 1523 (2019) merged the boards into TBPELS, the surveying Professional and Technical Standards were recodified into 22 TAC Chapter 138, Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.). Citing §663 is outdated for 2026.

content.alt: Diagram of a metes-and-bounds tract with a labeled Point of Beginning, perimeter calls, monuments, and tie to an original GLO survey corner.
content.caption: Figure: A closed metes-and-bounds tract tied to a corner of the original GLO survey/abstract, with bearing-distance calls and found/set monuments.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A metes-and-bounds description runs continuous bearing-distance calls around a closed perimeter back to the POB.
Texas captions tie to the original GLO survey + abstract number, not a PLSS grid.
Monuments control over bearings/distances; stated area is the least reliable element.
State the basis of bearing (22 TAC §138.91(c)); historic magnetic bearings cause rotation discrepancies.
Current standards are 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subch. E — never cite the repealed Ch. 663.

flashcards.definition: The defined origin of a metes-and-bounds perimeter run, conventionally tied to a corner of the original GLO survey/abstract so the tract can be relocated independently of the surveyor's work.
flashcards.hint_1: Where the perimeter starts and closes.

flashcards.definition: The identifier assigned to an original Texas land-grant survey by the General Land Office; the spine of Texas land identification, recited in nearly every Texas description caption.

The stated reference to which the courses of a survey are oriented — under 22 TAC §138.91(c), an identifiable monumented line or an established geodetic system such as the Texas State Plane Coordinate System (NAD83).

lessons.title: Week 5: Legal Descriptions — PLSS and Lot-and-Block
learning_objectives: Describe the federal PLSS township-range-section framework and why Texas does not use it.
Explain the lot-and-block (recorded-plat) description method and its use in Texas subdivisions.
Identify when a Texas metes-and-bounds split triggers the platting requirement.
key_takeaways: The PLSS rectangular system (township, range, section) is the federal grid used in most western states.
Texas never adopted the PLSS; it retained the Spanish/Mexican grant + abstract framework.
Lot-and-block descriptions reference a recorded subdivision plat by lot, block, subdivision name, and recording data.
Dividing a tract into a subdivision in Texas generally requires a recorded plat (Loc. Gov't Code §212.004).

content.html:
Three Ways to Describe Land

American descriptions take three principal forms: metes and bounds (a perimeter of bearing-distance calls, Week 4), the Public Land Survey System (PLSS) rectangular grid, and lot-and-block reference to a recorded plat. A competent surveyor reads all three, but in Texas you will draft metes-and-bounds and lot-and-block and will only encounter the PLSS when working across a state line or in conveyances copied from PLSS jurisdictions.

The PLSS Rectangular System

In the federal public-land states, land disposal was organized by the federal General Land Office (now the BLM) into a rectangular grid. From an initial point (a principal meridian crossing a base line), land is divided into townships roughly six miles square, located by township (north/south rows) and range (east/west columns). Each township is divided into 36 sections of about one square mile (640 acres), numbered 1–36 in a boustrophedonic (back-and-forth) pattern, then into aliquot parts (NE¼, S½, etc.). Lost PLSS corners are restored under the BLM Manual of Surveying Instructions using single- and double-proportionate measurement.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas entered the Union retaining its public lands, so its land was never surveyed under the federal PLSS. There is no township-range-section grid in Texas. Texas uses irregular metes-and-bounds tracts identified by original survey name + abstract number, administered by the GLO. The two systems are mathematically unrelated.

content.html:
Lot-and-Block (Recorded-Plat) Descriptions

When a developer subdivides land, the surveyor prepares a plat showing numbered lots within blocks, streets, easements, and dimensions, and records it with the county clerk. Thereafter a parcel can be described simply by lot, block, subdivision name, and the plat's recording data (e.g., "Lot 7, Block C, Oak Hollow Subdivision, according to the plat recorded in Vol. __, Page __, Plat Records, Travis County, Texas"). The recorded plat is the controlling document; the lots are created simultaneously, which (as you will see in Week 7) means excess or deficiency is apportioned among them rather than resolved by seniority.

content.text: Texas differs: Even a metes-and-bounds split can trigger Texas subdivision control. Loc. Gov't Code §212.004 ('Plat Required') states that a division of a tract into a subdivision includes a division 'made by using a metes and bounds description in a deed of conveyance,' so you cannot evade platting merely by avoiding a formal plat.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: PLSS = federal rectangular grid: initial point, township/range, 36 sections per township (~640 ac each).
Texas never adopted the PLSS; it uses original survey + abstract metes-and-bounds.
Lot-and-block describes a parcel by lot, block, subdivision name, and plat recording data.
Recorded-plat lots are created simultaneously, so excess/deficiency is apportioned, not resolved by seniority.
A metes-and-bounds split into a subdivision can still require a recorded plat (Loc. Gov't Code §212.004).

flashcards.definition: The federal rectangular survey system dividing public-domain land into townships (~6 mi square), located by township and range, each containing 36 sections of about 640 acres. Texas never adopted it.
A description that identifies a parcel by its lot and block within a named subdivision as shown on a plat recorded with the county clerk, citing the plat's recording reference.
A PLSS subdivision of a township, nominally one square mile (640 acres); 36 sections make up a township, numbered 1–36 in a back-and-forth pattern.

lessons.title: Week 6: The Texas Land System — Spanish and Mexican Grants
learning_objectives: Identify the four sovereigns of the soil and the instruments that severed Texas land from each.
Explain how Texas retained its public lands at annexation and the GLO's role.
Convert among Texas surveying units: vara, labor, and league.
key_takeaways: Every Texas title traces to Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas.
Texas retained its public lands at annexation in 1845; its land was never federal public domain.
The GLO (founded 1836) is custodian of grant records and still issues patents.
The Texas vara is fixed at 33⅓ inches; a labor ≈ 177 acres and a league ≈ 4,428 acres.

content.html:
The Four Sovereigns of the Soil

Every parcel of privately held Texas land traces backward through a chain of deeds to an original grant from a sovereign. There are four, in order: Spain (1716–1821), Mexico (1821–1836), the Republic of Texas (1836–1845), and the State of Texas (1845–present). A Spanish or Mexican grant is a title; a Republic or State grant is a patent. Either instrument severs the land from the sovereign, and every later deed runs from that first grantee. Perfected Spanish and Mexican titles remain valid today and the land they conveyed cannot be re-patented—sovereignty was already severed. The Republic distributed huge acreage through headright certificates; a first-class headright gave a resident head of family one league and one labor (about 4,605 acres).

sections.title: Annexation (1845) and the General Land Office
sections.content: Texas was an independent republic from 1836 to 1845. By the Joint Resolution for Annexing Texas (Mar. 1, 1845), Texas "retain[ed] all the vacant and unappropriated lands lying within its limits"—the federal government did not take the public domain. Texas kept well over 200 million acres and dedicated land revenue to the Permanent School Fund. The General Land Office (GLO), founded December 22, 1836, is the oldest existing Texas state agency; it maintains the grant archive (roughly 35.5 million land-grant records back to 1720, including the Spanish Collection of ~4,200 Spanish/Mexican grants) and still issues patents. This single fact—state, not federal, source of title—explains most of what makes Texas boundary law distinctive.

content.html:
Texas Surveying Units: Vara, Labor, League

Original Texas field notes are frequently written in varas. Texas law fixes the vara at exactly 33⅓ inches = 2.7778 ft (36 varas = exactly 100 ft). Area units scale up from it: a labor is 1,000,000 square varas (about 177.1 acres, the farming unit) and a league (legua/sitio) is 25,000,000 square varas (about 4,428.4 acres, the grazing unit, = 25 labors). A standard married head-of-family colonization grant was one league plus one labor (about 4,605.5 acres). A retracing surveyor must convert varas to feet (multiply by 2.7778, or divide by 0.36) and reconcile the historic magnetic/assumed bearing basis to a modern datum.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas kept the Spanish/Mexican grant system and never adopted the PLSS. Use the Texas vara of 33⅓ in; do not apply a foreign vara value (Spanish 'vara de Burgos' ~32.9 in, Mexican vara ~32.99 in) to a Texas grant, or computed acreages will drift.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Four sovereigns: Spain, Mexico, the Republic, the State; a 'title' (Spain/Mexico) or 'patent' (Republic/State) severs sovereignty.
Texas retained its public lands at annexation (1845); the land was never federal public domain.
The GLO (est. 1836) holds the grant archive and still issues patents.
Texas vara = exactly 33⅓ in (2.7778 ft); 36 varas = 100 ft.
Labor ≈ 177 ac; league ≈ 4,428 ac (= 25 labors); standard family grant ≈ 4,605 ac.

flashcards.definition: The four sources of original Texas land title: Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas. Every Texas chain of title runs back to one of them.
The historic Texas linear unit, fixed by Texas law at exactly 33⅓ inches (2.7778 ft); 36 varas equal exactly 100 feet. Common in original GLO field notes (abbreviated 'vrs').

Texas area units: a labor is 1,000,000 sq varas (~177 acres, farmland) and a league (sitio) is 25,000,000 sq varas (~4,428 acres, grazing land, = 25 labors). The standard family colonization grant was one league + one labor (~4,605 acres).

lessons.title: Week 7: Boundary Evidence and the Priority of Calls
learning_objectives: State the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and apply it to a conflicting description.
Explain the footsteps doctrine and the paramount rule of intent from Stafford v. King.
Distinguish sequential (senior/junior) from simultaneous (proportionate) conveyances.
key_takeaways: Dignity of calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > (adjoinder) > course > distance > quantity.
The hierarchy always yields to the parties' intent and the footsteps of the original surveyor (Stafford v. King).
Sequential conveyances are governed by senior/junior rights; simultaneous ones by proportionate apportionment.
A grantor cannot convey land already conveyed to a senior grantee; the junior takes the remainder.

content.html:
Dignity of Calls

When the calls within a single description conflict, the law ranks them by relative reliability—their "dignity"—highest to lowest: (1) natural monuments (rivers, creeks, lakes, rocks); (2) artificial monuments (marked trees, stakes, set iron, fences); (3) adjoinder (an adjacent, already-established survey line—a modern Texas refinement inserted here); (4) course (bearing); (5) distance; and (6) quantity / area (the weakest). The idea is that the more permanent and identifiable a call, the more it reflects what was actually on the ground when the line was run, and the harder it is to fabricate.

sections.title: Case: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
sections.content: Stafford v. King is the foundational Texas authority on both the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine. The Court explained: "Natural objects are mountains, lakes, rivers, creeks, rocks, and the like. Artificial objects are marked lines, trees, stakes, etc." and that "course and distance are the most unreliable calls…hence the general rule, that course and distance yield to natural objects." It summarized the controlling order as "1, natural objects; 2, artificial objects; 3, course and distance." Crucially, Stafford also anchors the footsteps rule: "The actual identification of the survey, the footsteps of the surveyor upon the ground, should always be followed, by whatever rule they may be traced." The hierarchy is only a rule of construction; it always yields to the intent apparent on the face of the grant and to the original surveyor's footsteps where they can be retraced.

content.html:
Intent and Footsteps Are Paramount

The dignity hierarchy is a subordinate tool used to find the parties' intent when the descriptive language conflicts. The cardinal Texas principle is that a retracing surveyor and the court must follow the footsteps of the original surveyor and give effect to the grant's intent, locating lines where they were actually run even against later "more accurate" measurements (Silver Oil & Gas, Inc. v. EOG Resources, Inc., 246 S.W.3d 197 (Tex. App.—San Antonio 2007)). A found, identifiable original monument controls over a conflicting field-note call. The exact location of a boundary line is ultimately a question of fact.

Sequential vs. Simultaneous Conveyances

How a discrepancy is resolved depends on how the parcels were created. Sequential conveyances—tracts carved out one after another over time—are governed by senior/junior rights: the earlier (senior) deed takes the full measure of what its description grants, and the later (junior) deed takes only the remainder; a grantor cannot convey land already conveyed to the senior grantee, so the junior absorbs any excess or eats any deficiency. Simultaneous conveyances—multiple lots created at one instant from a recorded subdivision plat—have no senior lot; excess or deficiency found between controlling monuments is apportioned proportionately among the lots according to their record lengths (single proportionate measurement). Apportionment is a last resort: found monuments and original corners control first.

content.text: Texas differs / caution: The single/double 'proportionate measurement' methods are PLSS/BLM-Manual constructs. No marquee Texas Supreme Court case adopts them by name; in Texas, apportionment is established common-law surveying practice and a genuine last resort behind found monuments and the original surveyor's footsteps.

content.alt: Diagram contrasting a sequential carve-out (senior tract whole, junior takes remainder) with a simultaneous platted block (excess apportioned across lots).
content.caption: Figure: Sequential conveyance (senior takes full description; junior takes the residue) vs. simultaneous plat (excess/deficiency apportioned across lots).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Dignity of calls: natural > artificial > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity.
Stafford v. King (1867) is the Texas root of both the dignity hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine.
Intent and the original surveyor's footsteps are paramount; the hierarchy only helps find them.
Sequential conveyances → senior/junior rights; simultaneous (plat) conveyances → proportionate apportionment.
Found monuments control first; proportioning is a last resort, not a Texas marquee holding.

flashcards.definition: The hierarchy ranking conflicting calls by reliability: natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course (bearing) > distance > quantity/area. It is a rule of construction that yields to intent and to the original surveyor's footsteps.

The principle (from Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)) that the lines and corners actually run and marked by the original surveyor control retracement, even over inconsistent field-note calls, where they can be ascertained.

In sequential conveyances, the earlier (senior) deed takes the full measure of its description and the later (junior) deed takes only the remainder; the grantor cannot convey what was already conveyed, so the junior absorbs excess or deficiency.

lessons.title: Week 8: Midterm Exam
learning_objectives: Synthesize Weeks 1–7: sources of law, estates, deeds, legal descriptions, the Texas land system, and the priority of calls.
Apply the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the four-corners/intent rule to scenario problems.
Self-assess readiness before advancing to retracement, water, and adverse-possession topics.
key_takeaways: The midterm covers Weeks 1–7: legal sources/courts, estates, deeds, descriptions, Texas land history, and boundary evidence.
Be able to trace a Texas chain of title to a sovereign and tie a description to an abstract number.
Be able to rank conflicting calls and explain why intent and footsteps override the mechanical hierarchy.
Remember the Texas-specific traps: §13.006 quitclaim window, §5.042 abolitions, Ch. 138 not Ch. 663.

content.html:
Midterm Scope and Strategy

The Week 8 midterm is a comprehensive checkpoint over the first half of the course (Weeks 1–7). Expect scenario questions that ask you to apply doctrine rather than recite it: trace a chain of title to its sovereign source, classify a deed and its warranties, identify the controlling call in a conflicting description, and explain how Texas law diverges from general common law. Treat each fact pattern the way a court does—find the intent first, then use the dignity-of-calls hierarchy only as a tie-breaker.

High-Yield Review

Concentrate your review on the points that are both heavily tested and Texas-specific. From Weeks 1–3: the hierarchy of Texas law, the split-apex court system, the §5.042 abolitions (Shelley's Case, Worthier Title—not destructibility), the narrowed implied covenants of §5.023, and the quitclaim/BFP rule with its 2021 §13.006 four-year cleansing window. From Weeks 4–7: the required elements of a metes-and-bounds description, the abstract-number anchor, why Texas has no PLSS grid, the vara/labor/league conversions, and the dignity of calls anchored in Stafford v. King with the footsteps doctrine.

content.text: Exam tip: Whenever a question presents conflicting calls, resolve it in this order — (1) parties' intent within the four corners, (2) footsteps of the original surveyor / found monuments, then (3) the dignity hierarchy. The mechanical hierarchy is the last step, not the first.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The midterm is application-focused over Weeks 1–7; reason like a court, not a flashcard.
Master the Texas-specific traps: §5.042, §5.023, §13.006, Ch. 138 vs. repealed Ch. 663.
Resolve call conflicts by intent → footsteps/monuments → dignity hierarchy.
Be fluent in vara/labor/league conversions and the four sovereigns of the soil.

flashcards.definition: §5.042 abolitions (not destructibility), §5.023 narrowed implied covenants, §13.006 four-year quitclaim cleansing window, 22 TAC Ch. 138 (not repealed Ch. 663), no PLSS grid in Texas, dignity of calls subordinate to intent and footsteps.
flashcards.hint_1: The points where Texas diverges from general common law.

flashcards.definition: Resolve conflicting calls by (1) the parties' intent within the four corners, (2) the footsteps of the original surveyor and found monuments, and only then (3) the dignity-of-calls hierarchy.

lessons.title: Week 9: Original Surveys vs Retracement Surveys
learning_objectives: Distinguish an original survey from a retracement (resurvey) and state what each may and may not do.
Explain the footsteps doctrine as the governing rule of Texas retracement.
Describe the GLO's role and the LSLS credential for original-grant boundary work.
key_takeaways: A retracement re-establishes existing boundaries; it does not create or move them.
The footsteps of the original surveyor control retracement, even over inconsistent field-note calls.
The dignity-of-calls hierarchy is invoked only when the footsteps cannot be retraced.
The GLO holds the original-grant records; original state-land boundary work requires a Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS).

content.html:
Two Fundamentally Different Jobs

An original survey creates boundaries that did not previously exist—subdividing a tract, laying out a new street, establishing the lines of a fresh grant. A retracement (resurvey) does something very different: it re-establishes the lines and corners of an existing survey. A retracement never creates or relocates a boundary; it recovers where the original line already is. Confusing the two is a classic error: a surveyor who "corrects" an original line to make the measurements close better has improperly moved a boundary and exposed everyone to litigation.

The Footsteps Doctrine Governs Retracement

In Texas retracement the controlling rule is the footsteps of the original surveyor: where the original surveyor's actual lines and corners on the ground can be ascertained, they control even over inconsistent field-note calls. The dignity-of-calls hierarchy (Week 7) is a fallback, invoked only when those footsteps cannot be retraced. As the San Antonio court put it in Silver Oil & Gas, Inc. v. EOG Resources, Inc., "the cardinal rule is that the footsteps of the original surveyor, if they can be ascertained, should be followed"; absent ascertainable footsteps you prioritize natural → artificial → course → distance. The location of a survey line is a question of fact (TH Investments, Inc. v. Kirby Inland Marine, L.P., 218 S.W.3d 173 (Tex. App.—Houston [14th Dist.] 2007)).

sections.title: The GLO and the LSLS credential
sections.content: Because Texas titles flow from original GLO surveys and patents rather than a uniform federal grid, retracing the original surveyor's actual work is the dominant Texas methodology. A surveyor retracing an original grant must consult the General Land Office record—patents, field notes, sketches, maps—to locate original grant boundaries and corners, then follow the footsteps on the ground. Original surveys of state land, and corrections to original grant boundaries, require a Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS), a higher GLO-issued credential than the RPLS. The Occupations Code (§1071.002) defines "state land surveying" to include "determining by survey the location or relocation of original land grant boundaries and corners" and filing field notes in the GLO.

content.text: Texas differs: PLSS states restore lost corners under the BLM Manual's proportionate-measurement rules on a federal grid. Texas retained and surveyed its own public domain, so Texas retracement centers on the original Texas surveyor's monuments and footsteps administered through GLO records, with proportioning used only as a last resort.

content.html:
Lost vs. Obliterated Corners

A key retracement distinction: an obliterated corner is one whose physical evidence is gone but whose position can still be recovered from collateral evidence—occupation lines, fences, witness testimony, ties to adjacent monuments. A lost corner is one that cannot be recovered by any acceptable evidence and must be re-established by measurement (proportioning, on a plat). The surveyor's duty is to exhaust the search for evidence of the original position before declaring a corner lost. Too many boundary disputes arise because a surveyor proportioned a corner that was merely obliterated and recoverable.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: An original survey creates boundaries; a retracement re-establishes existing ones — it never moves them.
The footsteps of the original surveyor control retracement, even over inconsistent field-note calls.
The dignity hierarchy is a fallback used only when footsteps cannot be retraced.
Distinguish obliterated corners (recoverable from collateral evidence) from lost corners (re-established by measurement).
The GLO holds original-grant records; original state-land boundary work requires an LSLS.

flashcards.definition: A survey that re-establishes the lines and corners of an existing original survey; it does not create or relocate boundaries. The footsteps of the original surveyor control.

An obliterated corner has no surviving physical evidence but its position is recoverable from collateral evidence (occupation, testimony, ties); a lost corner cannot be recovered by acceptable evidence and is re-established by proportionate measurement.

A Texas credential, higher than RPLS and issued through the GLO, required to survey land in which the State or Permanent School Fund has an interest, including locating or relocating original land-grant boundaries and corners.

lessons.title: Week 10: Riparian and Littoral Boundaries
learning_objectives: Distinguish riparian from littoral rights and navigable from non-navigable streams in Texas.
Explain the Texas gradient boundary method and the statutory 30-foot navigability rule.
Describe the dual coastal datum from Luttes v. State and the effect of accretion vs. avulsion.
key_takeaways: Riparian rights border flowing watercourses; littoral rights border static bodies (lakes, the Gulf).
A Texas stream is navigable if its bed averages 30 ft in width (Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3)); the State owns navigable beds.
The boundary of a navigable Texas stream is the gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd), not the ordinary high-water mark.
Coastal datum depends on the sovereign: Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water; Anglo/Texas grants use mean high tide (Luttes).

content.html:
Riparian vs. Littoral; Navigable vs. Non-Navigable

Riparian rights attach to land bordering a flowing watercourse (a river or stream); littoral rights attach to land bordering a static body (a lake, the sea, the Gulf). For a non-navigable stream the abutting owner's title runs to the center thread of the channel. For a navigable stream private title stops at the bank (the gradient boundary) and the State owns the bed, holding it in trust for the public. The classification therefore decides who owns the land under the water—a question the surveyor must answer before locating the line.

The 30-Foot Rule and State Ownership of Beds

Texas supplies a bright-line geometric test of navigability: a stream is navigable in fact (and thus public, with a State-owned bed) if it "retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up" (Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3), originating in an 1837 Republic act). The 30 feet is measured across the entire bed between banks, not the water flowing on any given day—so a channel that is sometimes dry can still be navigable. The State of Texas owns the beds of navigable streams, subject to the 1929 "Small Bill," which relinquished the bed surface to certain abutting owners while reserving the minerals to the State and preserving the public's rights in the water.

sections.title: Case: Motl v. Boyd — the gradient boundary
sections.content: The landward limit of the bed of a navigable Texas stream is the gradient boundary: a graded line along the bank midway between (i) the lower water level that just reaches the cut bank and (ii) the higher level that just does not overtop it—tied to the "cut bank" or "qualified bank." The Texas Supreme Court adopted this in Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926), defining the "bed" as the soil "alternately covered and left bare…at its average and mean stage during an entire year," excluding extreme freshets and droughts. The method was developed by surveyor Arthur A. Stiles for the Red River line in Oklahoma v. Texas, 260 U.S. 606 (1923), and refined in Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000) as the mid-height point of the lowest qualified bank in the vicinity. It is a difficult expert survey—do not equate it with a simple high-water line.

content.text: Texas differs: Most states fix the riparian boundary at the ordinary high-water mark (an observable vegetation/scour line). Texas instead uses a computed gradient line at the mid-height of the qualified bank, requiring water-level observation and bank profiling. The 30-ft average-width navigability test is also essentially unique to Texas.

content.html:
Coastal Boundaries: The Dual Datum and Avulsion

On the coast the seaward boundary of upland depends on the sovereign source of the grant (Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959)): a Spanish/Mexican grant is bounded by mean higher high water (MHHW)—the average of the higher of the two daily high tides over an ~18.6-year epoch, which pushes the line landward—while an Anglo-American (Republic/State) grant is bounded by mean high tide / mean high water. A coastal surveyor must first trace the chain of title to the original sovereign before choosing a datum. Finally, boundaries shift with gradual change (accretion adds land, erosion removes it) but are fixed by avulsion—a sudden, perceptible change such as a flood cutting a new channel does not move the boundary (and, under Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012), does not automatically "roll" a public beach easement onto formerly private land).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Riparian = flowing watercourse; littoral = static body. Non-navigable: title to the center thread; navigable: State owns the bed.
A Texas stream is navigable if its bed averages 30 ft wide (§21.001(3)); the State owns navigable beds.
The boundary of a navigable Texas stream is the gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard), not the OHWM.
Coastal datum follows the sovereign: Spanish/Mexican = MHHW; Anglo/Texas = mean high tide (Luttes v. State).
Gradual change moves the boundary; avulsion fixes it (Severance v. Patterson).

flashcards.definition: The Texas method for locating the landward limit of a navigable stream's bed: a graded line along the bank midway between the water level that just reaches the cut bank and the level that just fails to overtop it (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard v. State).
Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3): a 'navigable stream' is one that retains an average bed width of 30 feet from its mouth up. Such streams are public, with a State-owned bed.
From Luttes v. State (1959): the seaward boundary of upland is mean higher high water for Spanish/Mexican grants but mean high tide (mean high water) for Anglo-American (Republic/State) grants.

lessons.title: Week 11: Adverse Possession and Acquiescence
learning_objectives: Identify the five Texas adverse-possession limitation tracks and their distinct elements.
Apply the correct track to a fact pattern and compute when title ripens.
Explain how Texas treats encroachments, acquiescence, and the surveyor's role.
key_takeaways: Texas adverse possession is wholly statutory: Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code §§16.024–16.028 (3/5/10/25/25-year tracks).
The 5-year track (§16.025) requires paying taxes and a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed; one missed year is fatal.
The 10-year track (§16.026) needs no instrument but is capped at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded deed.
Encroachments mature into title only through the strict statutory tracks; Texas applies acquiescence narrowly.

content.html:
A Wholly Statutory, Multi-Track Scheme

Adverse possession in Texas is entirely statutory and lives in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16 (not the Property Code). Section 16.021 defines the key terms: adverse possession is "an actual and visible appropriation of real property, commenced and continued under a claim of right that is inconsistent with and is hostile to the claim of another person"; peaceable possession is continuous possession not interrupted by an adverse suit. Rather than one common-law test, Texas offers five distinct limitation periods, each with its own elements; a claimant pleads under the track whose requirements it can satisfy.

rows: Peaceable & adverse possession under title or color of title (strongest paper basis → shortest period)
Cultivate/use/enjoy + pay applicable taxes + claim under a duly registered deed (excludes quitclaim/forged)
Peaceable & adverse, cultivating/using/enjoying; no instrument/tax needed but capped at 160 acres unless enclosed or under a recorded deed fixing boundaries
Runs regardless of any legal disability (minority, unsound mind) — absolute long-stop
Under a recorded instrument purporting to convey, even if void on its face (excludes quitclaim/forged); extends to the whole area described

content.text: Texas differs: The §16.025 five-year track requires PAYING PROPERTY TAXES each year — a single missed year is fatal. This tax requirement is absent from traditional common law and is a frequent claim-killer. The §16.026 160-acre cap (absent enclosure or a recorded deed) also has no general common-law analog.

content.html:
Tacking, Encroachments, and Acquiescence

Tacking across successive possessors is allowed where they are in privity, but the statutory prerequisites (taxes under §16.025, a registered or recorded deed) are stricter and more formalized than common-law adverse possession. An encroachment—a fence, eave, building, or driveway intruding onto a neighbor—can mature into title only through these statutory tracks, or into a use-right by prescriptive easement (both hard to prove in Texas). Texas has no special "innocent improver" statute letting a good-faith encroacher simply buy the strip; title passes only through the statutory tracks, and Texas applies acquiescence / agreed-boundary doctrines narrowly. The surveyor's measured boundary is usually dispositive of where the line is—and therefore of whether an encroachment exists.

sections.title: Color of title vs. title (§16.021)
sections.content: "Title" means a regular chain of transfers from or under the sovereignty of the soil. "Color of title" means a consecutive chain that is not a regular chain from the sovereign, or a regular chain with a missing or defective link. The distinction drives the 3-year track (§16.024): the stronger your paper basis, the shorter the period the law requires before title ripens—hence the 3-year period is reserved for possession under title or color of title.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Texas adverse possession is wholly statutory: §§16.024–16.028 (3/5/10/25/25-year tracks), in the Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code.
The 5-year track requires paying applicable taxes and a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed.
The 10-year track needs no instrument but is capped at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded deed.
Tacking requires privity; encroachments mature into title only through the strict statutory tracks.
Texas has no innocent-improver statute and applies acquiescence/agreed-boundary doctrines narrowly.

flashcards.definition: Five statutory limitation periods in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16: §16.024 (3 yr, title/color of title), §16.025 (5 yr, taxes + registered deed), §16.026 (10 yr, 160-acre cap), §16.027 (25 yr, despite disability), §16.028 (25 yr, recorded but void instrument).

Under §16.025 the claimant must cultivate/use/enjoy the land, pay applicable property taxes each year, and claim under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed. A single missed tax year defeats the claim.

A consecutive chain of transfers that is not a regular chain from the sovereign, or a regular chain with a missing or defective link; it supports the shortest (3-year) adverse-possession track under §16.024.

lessons.title: Week 12: Easements and Rights-of-Way
learning_objectives: Distinguish express, implied (prior use), necessity, and prescriptive easements in Texas.
Explain why Texas disfavors prescriptive easements and channels access claims into necessity.
Apply the unity-of-ownership and strict-necessity elements to easement scenarios.
key_takeaways: Express easements (written, Statute of Frauds) are the most-favored method of creation in Texas.
Easement by necessity requires unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity, and necessity at severance; it ends when the necessity ends.
Texas disfavors prescriptive easements; joint/shared use defeats the exclusivity element (Tran v. Macha).
Hamrick v. Ward channels landlocked road-access claims into necessity, not prior use.

content.html:
What an Easement Is

An easement is a non-possessory right to use another's land for a specified purpose—access, utilities, drainage. The land benefited is the dominant estate; the land burdened is the servient estate. Easements are created in several ways, and Texas has strong preferences among them. Express easements—created by a written instrument satisfying the Statute of Frauds (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §26.01)—are the most-favored method, because Texas disfavors the implied and prescriptive routes. In practice, that means most Texas access rights are documented expressly rather than created by operation of law.

rows: Written grant or reservation (Statute of Frauds); most favored in Texas
Unity of ownership before severance; use open/apparent, continuous, reasonably necessary
Unity of ownership before severance; strict necessity (true landlock); necessity existed at severance
Open/notorious, continuous, adverse, AND exclusive use for 10 yr — disfavored in Texas

content.html:
Necessity vs. Prior Use After Hamrick v. Ward

An easement by necessity requires all three of: (1) unity of ownership of the dominant and servient estates as a single tract before severance; (2) access that is a present strict necessity, not a mere convenience (true landlock—no other legal access); and (3) necessity that existed at the time of severance. The easement terminates when the necessity ends (e.g., when alternative legal access becomes available). Unity of ownership is enforced rigorously: the grantor must have owned both estates "as a unit or single tract" (Koonce v. Brite Estate, 663 S.W.2d 451 (Tex. 1984); reaffirmed in Staley Family P'ship v. Stiles, 483 S.W.3d 545 (Tex. 2016)). In Hamrick v. Ward, 446 S.W.3d 377 (Tex. 2014), the Court held that a party claiming road access to a landlocked, previously unified parcel must proceed under necessity, not prior use—narrowing a doctrine unsettled in Texas for over a century. Prior use still applies to non-access uses (utility lines, drainage) where it remains reasonably necessary.

sections.title: Case: Tran v. Macha — prescriptive easements disfavored
sections.content: Texas does not favor prescriptive easements and scrutinizes every element. Besides being open/notorious, continuous, and adverse for the 10-year period, the use must be exclusive—and in Texas "exclusive" means use by the claimant and not by the landowner. Joint or shared use defeats the claim. In Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006), neighbors shared a driveway under a mutual mistake; the Texas Supreme Court reversed the easement, holding that mutual/joint use cannot support an exclusive, hostile claim—"joint use of a road, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement." Because most driveway and road-sharing fact patterns involve some owner use, Texas prescriptive claims rarely succeed, and access disputes are funneled toward necessity easements and express grants.

content.text: Texas differs (critical): Under the general common law / Restatement view an easement is non-possessory, so most states do NOT require exclusivity for prescription — shared road use can create one. Texas is the opposite: shared/joint use is normally fatal (Tran v. Macha). Texas also channels landlocked road access into the stricter necessity framework (Hamrick v. Ward).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Express easements (written, Statute of Frauds) are the most-favored creation method in Texas.
Easement by necessity: unity of ownership before severance + strict necessity + necessity at severance; ends when necessity ends.
Unity of ownership is enforced rigorously (Koonce; Staley).
Hamrick v. Ward channels landlocked road access into necessity, not prior use.
Texas disfavors prescriptive easements; joint/shared use defeats exclusivity (Tran v. Macha).

flashcards.definition: Requires (1) unity of ownership of dominant and servient estates as one tract before severance, (2) strict necessity (true landlock, not convenience), and (3) necessity existing at the time of severance. It terminates when the necessity ends.

In Texas a prescriptive easement requires exclusive use — use by the claimant and not the landowner. Joint or shared use, however long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement (Tran v. Macha, 2006).
Texas Supreme Court case (2014) holding that a claim of roadway access to a landlocked, previously unified parcel must proceed under easement by necessity, not implied easement by prior use.

lessons.title: Week 13: The Surveyor as Expert Witness
learning_objectives: Describe the surveyor's role and duties when testifying as an expert in a boundary dispute.
Explain that boundary location is a question of fact and how the surveyor's evidence informs it.
Apply professional standards to the preparation and presentation of expert opinions.
key_takeaways: The location of a boundary line is a question of fact for the factfinder; the surveyor supplies the evidence.
An expert surveyor must base opinions on the evidence (footsteps, monuments, record) and stay objective.
The surveyor testifies to where the boundary is; the court decides who owns the disputed land.
Thorough field notes, a defensible methodology, and the dignity-of-calls/footsteps analysis make credible testimony.

content.html:
From the Field to the Witness Stand

When a boundary dispute reaches court, the surveyor is frequently the central expert witness. The location of a survey line is a question of fact (TH Investments v. Kirby Inland Marine, 218 S.W.3d 173 (Tex. App.—Houston [14th Dist.] 2007)), and the factfinder—judge or jury—decides it on the evidence. The surveyor's job is to explain that evidence: what monuments were found and set, how the original surveyor's footsteps were traced, how the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the intent of the grant resolve the conflicting calls. The surveyor testifies to where the boundary is; the court decides who owns the disputed strip.

Duties of the Expert

An expert surveyor owes duties of competence, objectivity, and candor. Opinions must rest on adequate evidence—record research (GLO, deed records, plats), field recovery of monuments, and a defensible retracement methodology—not on advocacy for the retaining party. The surveyor must disclose the bases of an opinion, acknowledge contrary evidence and reasonable alternative interpretations, and resist pressure to shade the boundary toward the client. Credibility on cross-examination is built in the field months earlier, through complete field notes, photographs, and a documented chain of reasoning from evidence to conclusion. The surveyor must also remember the lane boundary: render opinions on boundary location and survey practice, not legal conclusions about title, which are for the court and counsel.

content.text: Practice point: An expert opinion is only as strong as its evidentiary foundation. Document every found and set monument, the record sources consulted, and the reasoning by which intent, footsteps, and the dignity of calls led to the located line — that record is what survives cross-examination.

sections.title: Limitations period for suits against surveyors
sections.content: Surveyors can be sued for negligent surveys, and Texas sets a specific limitations period for such suits in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code §16.011. This is one reason expert-quality documentation matters even on routine jobs: a defensible, well-recorded methodology both supports your testimony when you are the retained expert and protects you when your own work is challenged. Treat every boundary survey as potential exhibit evidence.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Boundary location is a question of fact; the surveyor supplies the evidence and the court decides.
An expert testifies to where the boundary is — not who owns the disputed land.
Expert opinions must rest on record research, monument recovery, and a defensible retracement method.
Objectivity and candor (acknowledging contrary evidence) build credibility on cross-examination.
Complete field documentation both strengthens testimony and guards against §16.011 surveyor-liability suits.

flashcards.term: Boundary location as a question of fact
flashcards.definition: Under Texas law (e.g., TH Investments v. Kirby Inland Marine) the location of a survey line is a fact question decided by the factfinder; the surveyor's role is to present the evidence and reasoning, not to decide ownership.

The obligation to base opinions on adequate evidence, disclose the bases of those opinions, acknowledge contrary evidence and reasonable alternatives, and remain objective rather than advocate for the retaining party.

lessons.title: Week 14: Professional Ethics and Texas Survey Standards
learning_objectives: Identify the governing statute and current administrative rules for Texas surveyors.
Apply the TBPELS standards of practice (22 TAC Ch. 138) including monumentation and descriptions.
Explain the RPLS/LSLS credentials and core ethical obligations in boundary work.
key_takeaways: Surveyor licensing flows from Occupations Code Ch. 1071; standards of practice are in 22 TAC Ch. 138 (Subch. E).
The old 22 TAC Ch. 663 was repealed and merged into Ch. 138 after the 2019 board merger — do not cite §663.
Only an RPLS (or LSLS for state land) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description and seal a survey.
TBPELS rules require honoring junior/senior rights, the record chain, and proper monumentation.

content.html:
Who Regulates Texas Surveyors

Texas surveyor licensing flows from the Occupations Code, Chapter 1071—the Professional Land Surveying Practices Act—which defines the credentials and sets qualifications and discipline. The regulator is the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS), created by HB 1523 (86th Legislature, 2019), which merged the former separate engineering and surveying boards into one. A Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) is licensed by TBPELS; only an RPLS (or, for state land, an LSLS) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description, delineate and monument boundaries, and seal and sign the survey.

The Standards of Practice — 22 TAC Chapter 138

The current Professional and Technical Standards are in 22 TAC Chapter 138, Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.), "Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors." Key sections: §138.83 (Precision and Accuracy—traverse and closure tolerances); §138.87 (Monumentation—monuments "set at sufficient depth to retain a stable and distinctive location" and traceable to the responsible registrant); and §138.91 (Survey Drawing / Written Description / Report—definite and unambiguous boundaries, stated basis of bearing, and identification of which monuments were found versus placed). A Category 1A land-title survey—the highest Texas standard—must certify substantial compliance with the current TSPS Manual of Practice and include a separate closure report.

content.text: Do not cite 22 TAC Chapter 663 (or Ch. 661). Those surveying standards were repealed and merged into Ch. 138 after the 2019 TBPELS consolidation. Citing §663 is outdated for 2026; the standards of practice are now in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E.

content.html:
Core Ethical Obligations

Beyond technical accuracy, a surveyor owes ethical duties that the TBPELS rules embody. Retracement work must honor junior/senior rights and the record chain—the surveyor follows the evidence to the legally correct line rather than to the line a client wishes existed. Monuments must be set and documented honestly (found vs. placed). The surveyor must practice only within the scope of the license, avoid the unauthorized practice of law (no title opinions), disclose conflicts, and protect public welfare—an inaccurate boundary harms not only the client but the neighbors and successors who rely on the record for generations. Competence, integrity, and independence are the through-line of the standards.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Licensing flows from Occupations Code Ch. 1071; the regulator is TBPELS (merged 2019).
Standards of practice are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subch. E (§§138.81 et seq.) — never cite repealed Ch. 663.
Only an RPLS (or LSLS for state land) may prepare descriptions and seal surveys.
Rules require honoring junior/senior rights, the record chain, and proper monumentation (§138.87).
Ethics: competence, integrity, independence, no unauthorized practice of law, protect public welfare.

flashcards.definition: The Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors, created by HB 1523 (2019) by merging the former engineering and surveying boards; it licenses surveyors and adopts the standards of practice in 22 TAC Ch. 138.

The current Texas administrative rules ('Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors'); Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.) holds the Professional and Technical Standards (precision §138.83, monumentation §138.87, descriptions §138.91). It replaced the repealed Ch. 663.

A Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) is the TBPELS license to practice land surveying; a Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS) is the higher GLO credential required to survey land in which the State or Permanent School Fund has an interest.

lessons.title: Week 15: Case Law Review and Comprehensive Exam Preparation
learning_objectives: Recall the leading Texas boundary-law cases and the rule each establishes.
Integrate doctrines across the course into a coherent boundary-resolution framework.
Identify the Texas-specific divergences most likely to be tested on the final and the SIT exam.
key_takeaways: Stafford v. King anchors the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine; both yield to intent.
Motl v. Boyd / Brainard give the gradient boundary; Luttes gives the dual coastal datum.
Tran v. Macha and Hamrick v. Ward show Texas disfavoring prescriptive easements and channeling access into necessity.
Adverse possession is statutory (§§16.024–16.028); standards are 22 TAC Ch. 138, not Ch. 663.

content.html:
A Coherent Framework

This course is really one connected argument. Texas titles trace to a sovereign (Spain, Mexico, the Republic, the State) and were never federal public domain, so Texas land is described by metes-and-bounds tied to a GLO survey and abstract, not a PLSS grid. When descriptions conflict, you resolve them by intent first, then the footsteps of the original surveyor and found monuments, and only then the dignity-of-calls hierarchy. Special boundaries (water, easements, adverse possession) layer Texas-specific rules on top of that base. The final exam rewards students who can move fluidly across these doctrines in a single fact pattern.

sections.title: Leading Texas cases — quick review
sections.content:
• Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867) — dignity of calls (natural > artificial > course/distance) and the footsteps doctrine; both yield to intent.
  • Motl v. Boyd, 286 S.W. 458 (Tex. 1926), refined by Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000) — the gradient boundary for navigable streams.
  • Luttes v. State, 324 S.W.2d 167 (Tex. 1959) — dual coastal datum (Spanish/Mexican = MHHW; Anglo/Texas = mean high tide).
  • Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006) — joint use defeats a prescriptive easement.
  • Hamrick v. Ward, 446 S.W.3d 377 (Tex. 2014) — landlocked road access goes through necessity, not prior use.
  • Koonce v. Brite Estate, 663 S.W.2d 451 (Tex. 1984) — unity of ownership for easement by necessity.
  • Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001) — possession as constructive notice must be visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal.
  • Geodyne v. Newton, 161 S.W.3d 482 (Tex. 2005) — quitclaim conveys the grantor's rights 'if any'; classification turns on the granting language.

rows: Texas is a pure NOTICE state (§13.001/§13.002): a later BFP without notice wins even if it records second or never
Grantee generally not a BFP; §13.006 (2021) adds a four-year cleansing window downstream
§5.042 abolished Shelley's Case and Worthier Title — but not destructibility
Gradient boundary on navigable streams; 30-ft navigability rule; dual coastal datum
Prescriptive disfavored (exclusivity / Tran v. Macha); necessity channeled by Hamrick
Statutory §§16.024–16.028; 5-yr track needs taxes; 10-yr capped at 160 acres
22 TAC Ch. 138 (not repealed Ch. 663); TBPELS since 2019

content.text: Final-exam strategy: For every scenario, first ask 'what is the controlling intent?' and 'whose footsteps/monuments govern?' before reaching for a mechanical rule. Then flag any Texas divergence (notice state, gradient boundary, statutory adverse possession, disfavored prescription, Ch. 138 standards).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Resolve boundary conflicts by intent → footsteps/monuments → dignity of calls.
Stafford v. King (calls + footsteps); Motl/Brainard (gradient boundary); Luttes (dual coastal datum).
Tran v. Macha and Hamrick v. Ward define Texas's restrictive treatment of prescriptive and prior-use easements.
Texas is a pure notice recording state; the quitclaim grantee is generally not a BFP (with the §13.006 window).
Adverse possession is statutory (§§16.024–16.028); current standards are 22 TAC Ch. 138.

flashcards.definition: Under Tex. Prop. Code §13.001/§13.002, Texas is a pure notice recording jurisdiction: a subsequent purchaser for value without notice of a prior unrecorded conveyance prevails — even if that purchaser records second or never records at all.
flashcards.hint_1: Recording order is irrelevant to the BFP's win.

flashcards.definition: Possession imparts constructive notice that defeats a bona fide purchaser only if it is 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' — a stricter four-adjective Texas formulation than the looser 'open and notorious' standard.

lessons.title: Week 16: Final Exam
learning_objectives: Demonstrate mastery of Texas surveying legal principles across the full course.
Apply integrated doctrine to novel boundary, water, easement, and adverse-possession scenarios.
Cite the correct Texas authority for each principle without relying on outdated rules.
key_takeaways: The final is comprehensive across all 14 instructional weeks and is application-focused.
Reason from intent and footsteps first; apply mechanical rules only as tie-breakers.
Cite current Texas authority: §§16.024–16.028, 22 TAC Ch. 138, §13.006 — not repealed or superseded rules.
The course prepares you for the legal-principles portion of the SIT and RPLS examinations.

content.html:
Final Exam Scope

The Week 16 final is a comprehensive, application-focused assessment over the entire course: sources of law and the court system; estates and deeds; legal descriptions (metes-and-bounds, PLSS, lot-and-block); the Texas land system and Spanish/Mexican grants; boundary evidence and the priority of calls; original versus retracement surveys; riparian and littoral boundaries; adverse possession; easements and rights-of-way; the surveyor as expert witness; and professional ethics and Texas standards. Expect multi-issue scenarios that ask you to integrate doctrine rather than recite isolated rules.

content.text: Reminder: This course supplies the legal foundation for the Surveyor-in-Training (SIT) and RPLS examinations. The final exam mirrors that style — apply current Texas authority, reason from intent and footsteps, and flag the Texas-specific divergences that distinguish this state from general common-law practice.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The final is comprehensive across all 14 instructional weeks and application-focused.
Reason from intent and footsteps first; mechanical rules are tie-breakers.
Cite current authority (§§16.024–16.028, 22 TAC Ch. 138, §13.006), not repealed rules.
This course feeds the legal-principles portion of the SIT and RPLS exams.

flashcards.definition: Covers all course topics: legal sources/courts, estates, deeds, descriptions, the Texas land system, priority of calls, original vs. retracement surveys, water boundaries, adverse possession, easements, expert testimony, and ethics/standards.
On the final and in practice, cite current Texas authority — adverse possession §§16.024–16.028, standards 22 TAC Ch. 138, quitclaim §13.006 — and avoid superseded rules such as 22 TAC Ch. 663.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boundary dispute over a residential lot line is filed in Texas. Through which court path will the case ultimately be resolved on a question of law?
options: District Court → Court of Appeals → Supreme Court of Texas
Justice Court → County Court → Court of Criminal Appeals
Municipal Court → Court of Appeals → Court of Criminal Appeals
County Court → Supreme Court of Texas → U.S. Supreme Court
quiz_questions.correct_answer: District Court → Court of Appeals → Supreme Court of Texas
quiz_questions.explanation: Boundary/title disputes are civil matters that begin in District Court, may be appealed to a Court of Appeals, and end at the Supreme Court of Texas. The Court of Criminal Appeals handles only criminal cases and never touches boundary precedent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks for the Texas adverse-possession statutes in the Property Code and cannot find them. Why?
options: Adverse possession is codified in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
Adverse possession is purely common law and has no statute in Texas
Adverse possession is in the Natural Resources Code with the GLO provisions
Adverse possession is in the Occupations Code with surveyor licensing
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Adverse possession is codified in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
quiz_questions.explanation: A classic Texas trap: adverse possession lives in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (§§16.021–16.028), not the Property Code. It is wholly statutory, not common law.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed conveys land 'to the City so long as it is used for a public park.' What estate and future interest does this create?
options: Fee simple determinable, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter
Fee simple subject to condition subsequent, leaving a right of re-entry
Life estate, leaving a reversion
Fee simple absolute, leaving no future interest
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Fee simple determinable, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter
quiz_questions.explanation: The durational phrase 'so long as' creates a fee simple determinable that automatically reverts on the stated event, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter. Conditional words like 'but if' would instead create a condition subsequent with a right of re-entry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which common-law rule did Tex. Prop. Code §5.042 NOT abolish?
options: The destructibility of contingent remainders
The Rule in Shelley's Case
The Doctrine of Worthier Title
The rule forbidding a remainder to the grantor's heirs
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The destructibility of contingent remainders
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 5.042 abolished Shelley's Case, Worthier Title, the remainder-to-grantor's-heirs rule, and a homestead-lien rule. It did NOT abolish destructibility of contingent remainders; Texas rejects that doctrine by case law instead.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas a conveyance is presumed to pass the entire fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Tex. Prop. Code §5.001 provides that a conveyance passes the grantor's whole estate unless the estate is limited by express words or a lesser estate is conveyed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A grantor signs a deed warranting title only against defects 'by, through, or under' himself. What deed is this, and what does the grantee receive?
