options: A special warranty deed; protection only against the grantor's own acts, not earlier links in the chain
A general warranty deed; protection against all defects back through the entire chain
A quitclaim deed; no warranty of any kind
A statutory form deed; an implied covenant of seisin that the grantor owns the land
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A special warranty deed; protection only against the grantor's own acts, not earlier links in the chain
quiz_questions.explanation: 'By, through, or under the grantor' is the hallmark of a special warranty deed: the grantor defends only against defects arising during his own ownership, not against earlier links. A general warranty defends the whole chain.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Tex. Prop. Code §5.023, the words 'grant' or 'convey' imply which covenants?
options: Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
A full covenant of seisin that the grantor actually owns the estate
A warranty against all defects in the entire chain of title
No covenants at all unless the deed says 'warrant and forever defend'
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 5.023 narrows the implied covenants to (1) no prior conveyance and (2) no encumbrances. Unlike the common law, it does not imply a covenant of seisin; broad protection requires the express 'warrant and forever defend' language of §5.022.

quiz_questions.question_text: A buyer pays value for land under a quitclaim deed recorded in 2023 and has no actual notice of a prior unrecorded claim. Two years later the prior claimant sues. What is the likely result under current Texas law?
options: The buyer is generally not a bona fide purchaser yet, because §13.006's cleansing window does not run until the fourth anniversary of recording
The buyer automatically wins as a bona fide purchaser because the quitclaim was recorded
The buyer wins because Texas treats a quitclaim grantee exactly like a warranty-deed grantee
The quitclaim is void and conveys nothing at all
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The buyer is generally not a bona fide purchaser yet, because §13.006's cleansing window does not run until the fourth anniversary of recording
quiz_questions.explanation: A Texas quitclaim grantee is generally not a BFP. Section 13.006 (2021) provides a cleansing window only after the FOURTH anniversary of recording; at two years the classic rule still applies, so the buyer takes subject to the prior claim.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a Texas metes-and-bounds description, which element is treated as the LEAST reliable when calls conflict?
options: Stated area/quantity (acreage)
A called natural monument such as a creek
A called artificial monument such as a set iron rod
The basis of bearing
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Stated area/quantity (acreage)
quiz_questions.explanation: Stated area is the weakest, least-reliable element — a check, not a control. Monuments (natural, then artificial) control over course and distance, and area yields to all of them.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a Texas description caption almost always recite an original survey name and abstract number?
options: Because Texas land is organized by original GLO land-grant surveys, not the federal PLSS grid
Because the abstract number identifies the PLSS section and township
Because federal law requires every deed to reference a principal meridian
Because the abstract number sets the basis of bearing for the survey
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because Texas land is organized by original GLO land-grant surveys, not the federal PLSS grid
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas land was never federal public domain and has no PLSS grid; it is identified by original survey + abstract number administered by the GLO. The abstract is the spine of Texas land identification.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor cites '22 TAC Chapter 663' as the source of the Texas standards of practice. Is this correct in 2026?
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Chapter 663 was repealed and merged into 22 TAC Chapter 138 (Subchapter E) after the 2019 board consolidation into TBPELS. Citing §663 is outdated for 2026.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about the Public Land Survey System (PLSS) and Texas is correct?
options: Texas never adopted the PLSS because it retained its public lands at annexation and uses original survey + abstract descriptions
Texas uses the PLSS township-range grid for all rural land
Texas adopted a modified PLSS administered by the GLO
The PLSS and the Texas abstract system are mathematically interchangeable
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Texas never adopted the PLSS because it retained its public lands at annexation and uses original survey + abstract descriptions
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas entered the Union retaining its public lands, so its land was never surveyed under the federal PLSS. There is no township-range grid in Texas; the two systems are unrelated.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lots in a recorded subdivision plat are created simultaneously. When a resurvey finds excess between the controlling block monuments, how is it handled?
options: It is apportioned proportionately among the lots; no lot is senior
The first lot sold takes all of the excess as the senior parcel
The last lot in the block absorbs the entire excess
The excess is returned to the original developer as reserved land
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is apportioned proportionately among the lots; no lot is senior
quiz_questions.explanation: Simultaneously created plat lots have no senior; excess or deficiency between controlling monuments is apportioned in proportion to record lengths. Senior/junior rights apply only to sequential conveyances.

quiz_questions.question_text: A South Texas chain of title bottoms out in an 1830 Mexican league-and-labor grant. Which statement is correct?
options: A perfected Mexican title remains valid and the land cannot be re-patented by the State
The Mexican grant was extinguished at annexation and must be re-patented
The grant is governed by the federal PLSS as adopted in 1845
The land reverted to the United States as federal public domain in 1845
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A perfected Mexican title remains valid and the land cannot be re-patented by the State
quiz_questions.explanation: Perfected Spanish/Mexican titles remain valid (recognized by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo and Texas constitutions). Because sovereignty was already severed, the land cannot be re-patented.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas land was part of the federal public domain and was surveyed by the federal General Land Office under the PLSS.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas retained its public lands at annexation in 1845; its land was never federal public domain. The Texas General Land Office (state, not federal) administers the grant records, and there is no PLSS grid in Texas.

quiz_questions.question_text: An original field note calls for a course of 'N 45° E, 300 ft to a marked oak,' but the marked oak is found 320 ft away on a bearing of N 47° E. The tree is unquestionably the original monument. Which call controls?
options: The marked oak (a found artificial monument), which controls over the course and distance
The course and distance, because measurements are more precise than a tree
The stated area of the tract, which is the highest-dignity call
Neither; the conflict voids the description
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The marked oak (a found artificial monument), which controls over the course and distance
quiz_questions.explanation: Under the dignity of calls (and Stafford v. King), a found, identifiable monument controls over conflicting course and distance. The footsteps of the original surveyor — here, the marked oak — locate the line.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which case is the foundational Texas authority for both the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine?
options: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
Motl v. Boyd, 286 S.W. 458 (Tex. 1926)
Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006)
Luttes v. State, 324 S.W.2d 167 (Tex. 1959)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
quiz_questions.explanation: Stafford v. King is the seminal Texas case stating the dignity of calls (natural > artificial > course/distance) and the footsteps doctrine. The other cases address water boundaries and easements.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor cannot find the physical evidence of an original corner but can recover its position from old fence lines, a witness's testimony, and ties to two adjacent original monuments. How should the corner be classified and re-established?
options: Obliterated — re-established from the collateral evidence, not by proportioning
Lost — re-established solely by proportionate measurement
Lost — abandoned and ignored in the retracement
Original — reset wherever the new measurements best close
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Obliterated — re-established from the collateral evidence, not by proportioning
quiz_questions.explanation: An obliterated corner has no surviving physical monument but its position is recoverable from collateral evidence, so it must be re-established from that evidence. Proportioning is reserved for genuinely lost corners after the search for evidence is exhausted.

quiz_questions.question_text: A retracement (resurvey) may correct and relocate an original boundary to make its measurements close more accurately.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: A retracement re-establishes existing boundaries; it never creates or relocates them. The footsteps of the original surveyor control even over inconsistent field-note calls.

quiz_questions.question_text: A creek's bed averages 35 feet in width from its mouth up, though it is dry several months a year. Under Texas law, who owns the bed?
options: The State of Texas, because the stream is navigable under the 30-foot rule (§21.001(3))
The abutting landowners to the center thread, because the creek is often dry
The federal government under the navigable-waters doctrine
Whoever recorded a deed to the bed first
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The State of Texas, because the stream is navigable under the 30-foot rule (§21.001(3))
quiz_questions.explanation: A stream whose bed averages 30 feet or more in width from the mouth up is navigable under Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3); the State owns the bed. The 30 feet is measured across the entire bed, so seasonal dryness is irrelevant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal tract derives from an 1829 Mexican grant. Which datum fixes its seaward boundary under Luttes v. State?
options: Mean higher high water (MHHW)
Mean high tide / mean high water (MHW)
Mean lower low water (MLLW)
The ordinary high-water mark used in PLSS states
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Mean higher high water (MHHW)
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Luttes v. State, Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by mean higher high water, which lies landward; Anglo-American (Republic/State) grants use mean high tide. The surveyor must trace the chain of title to the sovereign before choosing the datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas locates the boundary of a navigable stream at the ordinary high-water mark, the same observable vegetation line used in most other states.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas uses the gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard) — a computed line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank — not the ordinary high-water mark used elsewhere.

quiz_questions.question_text: A claimant has cultivated and enjoyed a tract under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed and has paid the property taxes every year except one, in which he forgot. Under the Texas 5-year track, what is the effect?
options: The claim under §16.025 fails because tax payment each year is a hard requirement and one missed year is fatal
The claim still ripens at five years because near-complete tax payment is sufficient
The claim converts automatically to the 3-year track under §16.024
The claim is unaffected because taxes are not required for adverse possession
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The claim under §16.025 fails because tax payment each year is a hard requirement and one missed year is fatal
quiz_questions.explanation: The §16.025 five-year track requires paying applicable property taxes each year; a single missed year defeats the claim. This tax requirement is distinctive to Texas and a frequent claim-killer.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the Texas 10-year adverse-possession track (§16.026), without an enclosure or a recorded deed fixing the boundaries, the claim is capped at how much land?
options: 160 acres
640 acres
40 acres
There is no cap
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 160 acres
quiz_questions.explanation: The §16.026 ten-year track is capped at 160 acres unless a larger area is enclosed or possession is under a recorded deed/memorandum fixing the boundaries. This acreage cap has no general common-law analog.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two neighbors share a gravel driveway for 20 years under a mutual mistake about the boundary; both use it regularly. One now claims a prescriptive easement. What result under Texas law?
options: No prescriptive easement, because joint/shared use defeats the exclusivity requirement (Tran v. Macha)
A prescriptive easement, because 20 years exceeds the 10-year period
A prescriptive easement, because exclusivity is not required for non-possessory rights
An easement by necessity, because the driveway is the only access
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No prescriptive easement, because joint/shared use defeats the exclusivity requirement (Tran v. Macha)
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas requires exclusive use for prescription — use by the claimant and not the owner. Under Tran v. Macha, joint use 'no matter how long' cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement, regardless of duration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A previously unified tract is now landlocked and the owner seeks roadway access across the grantor's retained land. Under Hamrick v. Ward, which doctrine must the owner use?
options: Easement by necessity (not implied easement by prior use)
Implied easement by prior use, which is easier to prove
Prescriptive easement after ten years of use
Express easement only; no implied route is available
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Easement by necessity (not implied easement by prior use)
quiz_questions.explanation: Hamrick v. Ward holds that a claim of roadway access to a landlocked, previously unified parcel must proceed under easement by necessity, not prior use. Prior use still applies to non-access uses like utilities.

quiz_questions.question_text: Testifying as a surveying expert in a boundary case, what is the proper scope of the surveyor's opinion?
options: Where the boundary is located, based on the evidence — not who legally owns the disputed strip
Both where the boundary is and who holds legal title to the land
Whether the deed is legally valid and enforceable
How the court should rule on the parties' competing claims
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Where the boundary is located, based on the evidence — not who legally owns the disputed strip
quiz_questions.explanation: Boundary location is a question of fact the surveyor's evidence informs, but the surveyor testifies to where the line is, not who owns the land or how the case should be decided — those are for the court and counsel.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which authority currently governs the Texas surveyor's standards of practice and the requirement to document which monuments were found versus placed?
options: 22 TAC Chapter 138, Subchapter E (TBPELS)
22 TAC Chapter 663 (the former surveying board)
The federal BLM Manual of Surveying Instructions
The Texas Property Code, Chapter 5
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 22 TAC Chapter 138, Subchapter E (TBPELS)
quiz_questions.explanation: Current standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subch. E, adopted by TBPELS; §138.91 requires identifying found versus placed monuments. Chapter 663 was repealed; the BLM Manual governs the federal PLSS, not Texas standards.

quiz_questions.question_text: Only a Registered Professional Land Surveyor (or an LSLS for state land) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description and seal the survey in Texas.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Occupations Code Ch. 1071, only an RPLS (or LSLS for land in which the State/PSF has an interest) may prepare descriptions, delineate/monument boundaries, and seal and sign the survey.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas is which type of recording jurisdiction with respect to a subsequent purchaser for value without notice?
options: A pure notice state — the later BFP without notice prevails even if it records second or never
A race state — the first to record always wins, even with notice
A race-notice state — the BFP wins only if it also records first
A first-in-time state with no recording statute
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A pure notice state — the later BFP without notice prevails even if it records second or never
quiz_questions.explanation: Under §13.001/§13.002 Texas is a pure notice state: a subsequent purchaser for value without notice of a prior unrecorded conveyance prevails, with no recording-priority requirement for the BFP.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Madison v. Gordon, possession imparts constructive notice that defeats a bona fide purchaser only if the possession is:
options: Visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal
Merely open and notorious, as in most states
Recorded with the county clerk
Continuous for at least ten years
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal
quiz_questions.explanation: Madison v. Gordon requires possession to be 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' to charge a purchaser with constructive notice — a stricter, four-adjective Texas test than the looser 'open and notorious' standard.

quiz_questions.question_text: Across the course, when a single description contains conflicting calls, in what order should a Texas surveyor resolve the conflict?
options: Intent of the parties → footsteps/found monuments → dignity-of-calls hierarchy
Dignity-of-calls hierarchy → stated area → intent
Distance → course → monuments → intent
Recording date → tax payment → dignity of calls
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Intent of the parties → footsteps/found monuments → dignity-of-calls hierarchy
quiz_questions.explanation: Intent within the four corners is paramount; next come the footsteps of the original surveyor and found monuments; the mechanical dignity-of-calls hierarchy is only the final tie-breaker.

problem_templates.name: Years to perfect Texas adverse possession (5-year track)
problem_templates.question_template: A claimant has held peaceable and adverse possession of a Texas tract, cultivating and using it, paying all applicable property taxes each year, and claiming under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed, since {{startYear}}. Under the Texas 5-year limitation track (Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code §16.025), in what calendar year does the limitation period first ripen into perfected title?

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the track
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Registered deed + taxes paid each year + use = the 5-year track (§16.025)

problem_templates.explanation_template: Because the claimant holds under a duly registered deed, uses the land, and pays taxes each year, the 5-year track (§16.025) applies. Title ripens 5 years after possession began: {{startYear}} + 5 = {{_answer}}.

problem_templates.name: Single-proportion correction of lot frontage in a platted block
problem_templates.question_template: A block of {{n}} lots was platted simultaneously, each with a record frontage of {{rec:f2}} ft (record total {{recTotal:f2}} ft). A resurvey between the two controlling original block-corner monuments measures an actual total of {{meas:f2}} ft. Using single proportionate measurement, what corrected frontage (ft) should each equal lot receive?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Plat lots are created simultaneously, so the measured surplus/deficiency is distributed proportionately to record frontage. With equal record lots, each corrected frontage is the measured total divided by the number of lots: {{meas:f2}} ÷ {{n}} = {{_answer}} ft.
